serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cherry tomatoes is approximately 1 cup (around 10–12 whole cherry tomatoes), weighing about 149 g.

nutrient_source.title: Cherry Tomatoes, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cherry tomato (raw). Input record confidence 0.95 with HPB provenance retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cherry tomato GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tomato (raw, GI ~15, low category). Cherry tomatoes are compositionally equivalent to standard tomatoes for GI purposes (similar carbohydrate content ~5g/100g, high water content, high fibre). GL per serving calculated as: GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (149g × 5.2g/100g = 7.75g) / 100 = 1.16, rounded to 1.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6 mg/100g) and high potassium (~237 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Cherry tomatoes are an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals due to their very low sodium content (6 mg/100g) and meaningful potassium contribution (~237 mg/100g), supporting the DASH dietary pattern for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), low carbohydrate (5.2 g/100g), and dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cherry tomatoes are highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; their low GI (~15), modest carbohydrate load (5.2 g/100g), and dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) result in minimal postprandial blood glucose elevation and a glycemic load of approximately 1.2 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat, high lycopene and vitamin C, low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cherry tomatoes are cardioprotective: they contain negligible fat (0 g/100g), no cholesterol, very low sodium (6 mg/100g), and are rich in lycopene and vitamin C, antioxidants associated with reduced LDL oxidation and cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade classification; solanine alkaloid content may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cherry tomatoes belong to the nightshade family (Solanaceae) and contain solanine alkaloids; RA patients with documented nightshade sensitivity should limit or avoid consumption and monitor joint symptoms, while those who tolerate them may benefit from the anti-inflammatory lycopene and vitamin C content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cherry tomato (Lycopersicon esculentum var. cerasiforme) is a small, round to oval variety of tomato typically 2–3 cm in diameter, characterised by a sweet, tangy flavour and juicy flesh when fully ripe. It is consumed raw as a snack, in salads, on skewers, or as a garnish, and is also used roasted or blistered in cooked dishes. Nutritionally, cherry tomatoes are very low in calories (22 kcal/100g), contain negligible fat (0 g/100g), modest protein (0.9 g/100g), and low carbohydrates (5.2 g/100g) with 3.5 g of natural sugars and 1.7 g of dietary fibre. They are an excellent source of vitamin C (~19 mg/100g), vitamin A (~42 mcg RAE/100g), potassium (~237 mg/100g), and the antioxidant lycopene, which gives them their characteristic red colour. Their glycemic index is very low (~15), making them suitable for blood glucose management. They are naturally sodium-free in practical terms (6 mg/100g), supporting DASH-diet and hypertension management goals. Cherry tomatoes are vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. They are classified as nightshade vegetables (Solanaceae), which is clinically relevant for rheumatoid arthritis patients with solanine sensitivity. Widely available year-round in Singapore and globally, they are a nutrient-dense, low-calorie whole food suitable for most dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Are cherry tomatoes good for diabetes?
How many calories in cherry tomatoes?
Can I eat cherry tomatoes if I have high blood pressure?
Are cherry tomatoes suitable for a low-calorie diet?
Cherry tomato vs regular tomato nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a reported confidence of 0.95. The input record is marked as reviewed and canonical. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the compositionally equivalent standard tomato entry, which is a well-established analogous match.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database for raw cherry tomato; cherry tomatoes are a naturally very low-fat food and 0.0 g/100g is the reported value. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant food. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero as this is a plant-based whole food with no animal-derived components. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown beyond the HPB Foodo reported fields is not available in the HPB Singapore database entry for cherry tomato as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, NDB 11529) reports these values and may be used for supplementary reference. potassium_mg (237 mg/100g), vitamin_c_mg (19.1 mg/100g), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (42 mcg/100g), and water_g (93 g/100g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11529, Tomatoes, cherry, raw) as these fields were not reported in the HPB source. The food_group in the input record is listed as 'Fruits' (reflecting botanical classification); this record uses 'Vegetables' to reflect culinary and nutritional database convention consistent with HPB and USDA classification. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for raw tomato; no cherry-tomato-specific GI entry was identified in the database as of 2026-04-16.
